source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Thunder Hawk Platform Pollution Event

event.top_event: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
actions_taken: Collection of overboard water samples within two hours of sheen observation
Manual adjustment of water levels in FWKO and Float Cell
Manual level control in vessels throughout the night
Controlled shut-in of production on May 15, 2018
Third controlled shut-in on May 16, 2018 as ordered by BSEE
Issuance of two Notifications of Incident of Noncompliance (INCs)
event.summary: On May 16, 2018, a pollution event occurred at Noble Energy's Mississippi Canyon 736 A (Thunder Hawk) Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident involved the discharge of approximately 14.77 barrels of oil from the facility's overboard water line. The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.
recommendations: Update start-up procedures and quality control checks to ensure proper sequencing of subsea well ramp-ups to account for large volumes of chemicals
Increase communication between offshore operations and onshore production leadership
Review and revise produced water handling system procedures to account for increased water production
Implement proper sequencing of well startups from unplanned shutdowns

hazards.name: Increased water production without system review
Unplanned production shut-in and restart cycles
Chemical injection (methanol and LDHI) into subsea flowlines
Inadequate level control in produced water handling vessels

threats.name: Inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences
Insufficient level sensor accuracy due to fluid specific gravity changes
Inadequate knowledge of equipment operations by personnel
Lack of communication between offshore and onshore operations

consequences.name: Unauthorized discharge of oil into offshore waters
Environmental pollution and marine ecosystem impact

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) procedures
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to ensure operational changes are reviewed and procedures updated
controls.lod_basis: Preventive control to identify need for procedure updates when operational conditions change

supporting_text: From the end of 2017 to the time of the incident, water production increased by approximately 6,000 barrels per day (BPD). However, the increase in water production did not prompt a review of the produced water handling system and well start up procedures.
Management Systems- Inadequate Management of Change (MOC) procedures: The change in water production did not prompt a review of the produced water handling system.

controls.name: Well start-up procedures and sequencing
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to ensure proper sequencing of well start-ups to prevent process upset
controls.lod_basis: Preventive control to manage fluid composition and level control during production ramp-up

supporting_text: A review of operational procedures and start-up procedures in particular may have identified the need to sequence well startups from unplanned shutdowns. Prior to the incident, the facility started their highest oil producing wells first.

The updating of start-up procedures and quality control checks to ensure proper sequencing of subsea well ramp-ups to account for large volumes of the chemical would help set a clearer expectation for operations and maintenance staff

controls.name: Level sensors and instrumentation
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to accurately measure fluid levels in process vessels
controls.lod_basis: Detection and monitoring control for process upset conditions

supporting_text: Operators stated that due to the ramp-up sequence, insufficient water inventory could have caused the produced oil and high volumes of methanol and LDHI to affect the specific gravity of the fluid inside of the process vessels. This may have caused the level sensors to have an inaccurate reading of the true fluid level.
These chemicals are water soluble and change the Specific Gravity of the fluid, which could result in inaccurate readings on the level sensors.

controls.name: Manual level verification and adjustment by deck operators
controls.barrier_role: Administrative/manual control to prevent oily water discharge when automated systems fail
controls.lod_basis: Compensatory control when level sensors provide inaccurate readings

supporting_text: While the deck operators verified the levels in the Free Water Knockout (FWKO), they discovered oily water in the produced water weir. The deck operators manually adjusted the water level in the FWKO to prevent oily water from discharging to the float cell.
The level was controlled manually in these vessels throughout the night until the next morning.

controls.name: Sheen observation and reporting procedures
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to detect and report pollution events
controls.lod_basis: Detection and notification control for environmental compliance

supporting_text: On May 15, 2018 at 0800 hours, Deck Operator 1 observed a 25 feet by 1,500 feet sheen (silvery light colored) emanating from the overboard water discharge on the southeast corner of the facility.

The OIM instructed the deck operators to initiate a collection of an overboard water sample within two (2) hours of first observation of the sheen and additional samples within the first twenty-four (24) hours of sheen observation to submit to a third-party lab, per National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit requirements.

controls.name: Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) system
controls.barrier_role: Engineering control to maintain power to critical control systems
controls.lod_basis: Preventive control to avoid uncontrolled shut-in events

supporting_text: On May 13, 2018, the first shut-in occurred. At approximately 1700 hours, a capacitor failure in the uninterruptible power supply (UPS) activated the smoke alarm, dropped power to the UPS system, and prompted a full uncontrolled shut-in of production including closure of the Surface Control Subsurface Safety Valves (SCSSVs).

controls.name: Communication between offshore and onshore operations
controls.barrier_role: Administrative control to ensure operational decisions are informed by production leadership
controls.lod_basis: Preventive control to ensure adequate procedures and oversight

supporting_text: With increased communication between offshore operations and onshore production leadership, the operational and reporting causal factors could be eliminated.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
